name: Padel Tennis
tagline: The fastest growing racket sport in the UK

seo.title: Best Beginner Padel Tennis Kit UK 2025 | KitUp
seo.description: Get started with padel tennis in the UK with the right racket, balls, and gear. Beginner kit recommendations with Amazon UK links.
keywords: best padel racket for beginners UK

intro: Padel is the fastest growing sport in the UK and it's not hard to see why. It combines the social ease of doubles tennis with the enclosed walls of squash, producing a game that's immediately fun for beginners and deeply tactical for experienced players. The court is smaller than a tennis court, the scoring is the same, and the glass walls are in play, which means rallies last longer, beginners look better than they are, and the learning curve is gentle. New courts are opening across the UK every week. If you've never tried it, book a taster session at your nearest club first. Most people are hooked within the hour.

kits.label: Starter Kit
kits.description: The essentials to get on court and start playing padel.

products.name: Head Padel Alpha Pro Racket
products.description: A round-shaped beginner padel racket from Head, one of the sport's most established brands. The round shape gives a large sweet spot centred lower on the face, which is more forgiving for beginners who are developing consistent contact. Made with fibreglass face and EVA rubber core.
products.why_we_picked: The shape of a padel racket affects where the sweet spot is and how forgiving it plays. Round rackets have the sweet spot in the middle of the face, which is where a beginner hits most consistently: it makes off-centre shots more playable rather than flying off wildly. Head's Alpha range is the standard entry-level recommendation at clubs across the UK. It's the racket most beginners hire for their first few sessions, and buying your own means you know exactly what you're getting.

products.name: Head Padel Pro S Balls (3-Pack)
products.description: Official padel balls from Head, slightly depressurised compared to tennis balls with a felt exterior. Sold in tubes of three: the right quantity for padel play.
products.why_we_picked: Padel balls are not the same as tennis balls. They're slightly softer and lower pressure, which controls the bounce off the walls and gives the game its distinctive rhythm. Using tennis balls on a padel court produces an unpredictable, faster game that doesn't suit the sport. Head Padel Pro balls are used on professional tours and widely available in the UK. A tube of three covers a session and the balls last several sessions before needing replacement.

products.name: Padel Racket Cover / Protective Case
products.description: A padded neoprene or polyester protective case for a single padel racket, with a zip closure and carry strap. Protects the racket face and handle from knocks and scratches between sessions.
products.why_we_picked: Padel rackets are solid (no strings to restring) but the face surface can crack or chip if knocked against the court walls, floor, or other rackets in transit. A padded cover is cheap insurance. Most padel rackets don't come with one included, and a scraped or cracked face affects both playability and resale value.

products.name: Wilson Padel Pro Overgrip (12 Pack)
products.description: Thin self-adhesive overgrip tape in a multi-pack. Wraps over the existing grip to improve feel, absorb sweat, and maintain control. Compatible with all padel and tennis racket handles.
products.why_we_picked: Grip condition makes a real difference to how securely you hold the racket during powerful shots and wall play. Overgrips are cheap, easy to apply, and transform a slippery or worn grip into something that feels solid. A pack of 12 keeps you going for months, and changing the grip takes two minutes. This is the first accessory that regular players always have in their bag.

kits.label: Level Up Kit
kits.description: A more advanced racket and proper padel bag for when you're playing regularly.

products.name: Babolat Technical Viper Padel Racket
products.description: An intermediate-level diamond-shaped padel racket from Babolat. The diamond shape places the sweet spot higher on the face, rewarding aggressive overhead play and smashes while still being accessible to improving players. Carbon fibre face with a textured surface for added spin.
products.why_we_picked: After a few months of regular play, a round beginner racket starts to feel limiting, particularly for overhead smashes and net play where you want power. The Babolat Viper moves the sweet spot higher, rewards more technical shots, and has the stiffer carbon face that more confident players prefer. It's a natural step up rather than a leap into professional equipment.

products.name: Head Tour Team Padel Monstercombi Bag
products.description: A dedicated padel bag with compartments for two rackets, a main gear compartment for clothing and shoes, a smaller accessories pocket, and padded shoulder straps. Fits all padel racket sizes.
products.why_we_picked: Once you're playing two or three times a week, carrying your racket in a case and your kit in a separate bag becomes annoying. A proper padel bag keeps everything together: racket protected, shoes separate from clothing, balls and overgrips in a side pocket. Head's bags are used on the World Padel Tour and are well-made and practical. The Monstercombi has enough space for everything you need without being cumbersome.

checklist: Comfortable sportswear you can move freely in
Water bottle (padel sessions are more intense than they look)

faqs.question: What is padel and how is it different from tennis?
faqs.answer: Padel is a racket sport played on an enclosed court roughly a third the size of a tennis court, surrounded by glass walls and metal fencing. It's almost always played in doubles. The glass walls are in play: you can hit the ball off the back wall to keep a rally going, similar to squash, which is what makes it so accessible to beginners and so addictive to experienced players. The scoring system is identical to tennis. The main differences: the court is smaller, the racket is solid (no strings), the ball is slightly lower pressure, and underarm serves are mandatory.

faqs.question: Do I need to be good at tennis to play padel?
faqs.answer: No. While a tennis background helps with understanding the scoring and some of the racket mechanics, padel is a genuinely different sport. Many padel players have little or no tennis experience. The smaller court and allowed wall play make rallies easier to sustain, which means beginners can have fun from the very first session rather than spending half their time chasing balls they've mishit. If anything, some tennis players find padel initially frustrating because the instinct to hit hard works less well than in tennis.

faqs.question: What shoes should I wear for padel?
faqs.answer: You need court shoes with non-marking soles, the same as for indoor tennis, squash, or badminton. The ideal padel shoe has a herringbone or omni-surface tread pattern for grip on the artificial grass that covers most padel court floors. Avoid running shoes (poor lateral support and wrong tread pattern) and avoid shoes with black soles (they mark the court surface). Most clubs will turn you away in unsuitable footwear. Padel-specific shoes exist from brands like Adidas, Head, and Babolat, or an indoor tennis shoe works equally well.

faqs.question: How do I find a padel court near me in the UK?
faqs.answer: Padel court numbers in the UK have grown rapidly since 2020. The Padel England website has a court finder covering clubs across England. Many leisure centres, tennis clubs, and dedicated padel venues now have courts, and most offer beginner sessions, taster days, and coaching. Urban areas now often have multiple options: London, Manchester, Birmingham, and Edinburgh all have extensive padel infrastructure. Many courts can be booked by the hour via the Playtomic app.

faqs.question: What's the difference between a round, teardrop, and diamond racket?
faqs.answer: The shape determines where the sweet spot is located. Round rackets have the sweet spot in the centre of the face: the most forgiving position, ideal for beginners. Teardrop rackets are a middle ground: the sweet spot is slightly higher, offering more power on overhead shots while remaining reasonably forgiving. Diamond rackets have the sweet spot high on the face, giving maximum power for smashes and volleys, but requiring more precise technique to use effectively. Beginners should start with round; move to teardrop as technique develops; diamond is for advanced players.

faqs.question: How do padel serves work?
faqs.answer: Padel serves are underarm only: no overhead serves like in tennis. Stand behind the service line in the service box, bounce the ball once, and hit it below waist height. The ball must land in the diagonally opposite service box. It can hit the back wall after bouncing in the service box without being a fault. If it hits the side walls before the service box, it's a fault. The server gets two attempts, exactly like tennis. The underarm serve rule is what keeps the sport accessible. A powerful overhead serve would make it much harder for beginners to play.

faqs.question: Is padel expensive to play in the UK?
faqs.answer: Court hire typically runs £15-£25 per hour split between four players, usually £4-£6 each, similar to badminton or squash court hire. Most clubs offer off-peak rates which can reduce this further. A beginner racket costs £40-£70 and lasts for years with reasonable care. Balls are around £6-£10 for a tube of three and last several sessions. Padel is broadly comparable to regular tennis in ongoing cost, and cheaper than golf. Coaching sessions for beginners are available at most clubs for £10-£15 per person in a group lesson.
